text: The surgeon lifts a large fibroid, approximately eight to nine centimeters, from behind the uterus. A chemical is injected at the junction of the fibroid and uterus, causing the uterus to turn white and reducing blood supply. The outer capsule of the fibroid is incised, revealing the fibroid with a thick capsule. A myoma screw is inserted to apply traction, allowing the surgeon to peel off the capsule and expose more of the fibroid.

The surgeon uses an electric current to cut the remaining fibers of a large fibroid from the right side of the uterus. A smaller fibroid located behind the uterus is also removed. The posterior flap of the capsule appears large, and the surgeon trims it to fit the anterior flap, ensuring no excess tissue remains. The outer layer of the capsule is stitched in two layers to prevent dead spaces and blood collection.

The surgeon cleans the inside of the surgical area and identifies a small fibroid measuring two to three centimeters located on top of the uterus. The outer capsule of the fibroid is incised, and the fibroid is pulled and peeled away from the capsule using traction. The bed of the fibroid is sutured to ensure the margins are opposed, leaving no dead spaces, facilitating proper healing.

The surgeon uses a morcellator to shred a large fibroid into small pieces, with the blade rotating to cut long threads of the fibroid capsule. The fibroid pieces are carefully extracted through a small incision, ensuring the sharp blade only contacts the fibroid tissue. The uterus is shown restored to its shape after the removal of the fibroids.

The surgeon thoroughly cleans the uterus, peritoneal cavity, ovaries, fallopian tubes, and abdominal area, removing all blood, debris, and small tissue fragments. The surgical field is repeatedly washed with normal saline to ensure a clean pelvis at the end of the procedure.
